facts.size: About 25 cm — similar to a small pigeon
facts.habitat: Chaparral, oak woodland, and open scrubby areas

hints.region: The state bird of California — common in chaparral, parks, and suburban gardens across the western USA
hints.size: About 25 cm — compact and round, similar in size to a small pigeon
hints.behavior: Moves in coveys (family groups) on the ground; the distinctive teardrop-shaped topknot is actually a cluster of six feathers that curves forward over the bill

funFact: California Quail chicks leave the nest within hours of hatching and can run and feed themselves almost immediately, though parents continue to brood them at night.

facts.size: About 10 cm — slightly larger than most other North American hummingbirds
facts.habitat: Coastal scrub, chaparral, open woodland, and suburban gardens
facts.diet: Nectar from flowers, tree sap, and small insects
facts.migration: Non-migratory; year-round resident along the Pacific Coast

hints.region: Common year-round along the Pacific Coast of North America, particularly in California — the most widespread hummingbird on the west coast
hints.size: About 10 cm — tiny even by hummingbird standards, weighing around 4 grams
hints.behavior: Males perform a dramatic dive display — climbing over 30 metres then diving nearly straight down past the female, making a loud chirp with their tail feathers at the bottom of the arc

funFact: Anna's Hummingbirds sing — making them unusual among hummingbirds; males perch and sing a buzzy, complex song year-round.

facts.size: 109–140 cm; wingspan up to 3 m — the largest flying bird in North America
facts.habitat: Rocky scrubland, coniferous forests, and canyon country
facts.diet: Carrion — almost exclusively large dead animals

hints.region: Found in the mountains and canyons of California, Arizona, Utah, and Baja California — an iconic conservation success story brought back from the brink
hints.size: The largest flying land bird in North America — wingspan up to 3 metres, the width of a small car
hints.behavior: Soars on thermal updrafts for hours without flapping, covering enormous distances to find carrion; bald head is an adaptation for feeding inside large carcasses

funFact: By 1987 only 27 California Condors remained alive — all were captured for a captive breeding programme that has since restored the wild population to over 500 birds.

facts.habitat: Near water — streams, ponds, and coastal areas with overhanging vegetation

hints.region: Common year-round near water in California and the southwestern USA; expands through Central and South America
hints.size: Small — about 17 cm, roughly sparrow-sized, and sits very upright on exposed perches near water
hints.behavior: Sallies out from a low perch to snap flying insects out of the air, always returning to the same perch — and bobs its tail up and down constantly

funFact: Black Phoebes often build their mud nests under bridges and eaves — the same individual will frequently return to the same nest site year after year.

facts.habitat: Grasslands, prairies, meadows, and agricultural fields
facts.diet: Insects in summer; seeds and grains in winter
facts.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter

hints.region: Common across western North America from Canada to Mexico — the state bird of six western US states
hints.size: About 23 cm — robin-sized, plump, and short-tailed when perched on a fence post
hints.behavior: Sings one of the most celebrated bird songs in North America — a rich, flute-like bubbling from open fence posts and tall grass stems

funFact: The Western Meadowlark's song is so complex that it has up to 12 different song types and can sing them in different orders to avoid sounding repetitive.
